chapter_summary: This chapter introduces the Yoga Sutras of Patañjali, beginning with the definition of yoga as the cessation of mental modifications (citta-vṛtti-nirodha). It outlines the five types of vṛttis (valid cognition, error, verbal knowledge, sleep, memory) and the means to their cessation through practice (abhyāsa) and dispassion (vairāgya). The chapter also describes the stages of samādhi, including samprajñāta and asamprajñāta, and the role of Īśvara-praṇidhāna.

story.title: Yoga Sutras: Samādhi Pāda
story.arc: Introduction to the philosophy and practice of yoga, defining key terms and outlining the path to samādhi.
story.timeline_note: Classical yoga text, likely composed around 400 CE.

teachings.teaching: Yoga is the cessation of the modifications of the mind.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this means stilling the vṛttis through breath control and meditation to realize the true Self.

teachings.teaching: The modifications are fivefold: valid cognition, error, verbal knowledge, sleep, and memory.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that these vṛttis must be observed and transcended through Kriya practices.

teachings.teaching: Cessation of vṛttis comes through practice and dispassion.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga integrates abhyāsa (regular practice of Kriya) and vairāgya (detachment from sensory objects).

teachings.teaching: Practice becomes firm when cultivated for a long time, without interruption, and with devotion.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma stresses the importance of consistent daily Kriya practice over many years.

teachings.teaching: Higher dispassion arises from the vision of Puruṣa, leading to non-thirst for the guṇas.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the realization of the Self beyond the three guṇas through advanced meditation.

teachings.teaching: Samādhi can be attained through surrender to Īśvara.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma views Īśvara-praṇidhāna as devotion to the cosmic consciousness, which Kriya-Yoga facilitates through mantra and meditation.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Yoga Sutras (Chapter 0, part 2) focuses on the concept of Īśvara (the Lord) and the practice of Īśvarapraṇidhāna (surrender to God) as a means to attain samādhi. It describes Īśvara as a special Puruṣa untouched by afflictions and karma, the seed of omniscience, and the teacher of the ancients. The praṇava (Oṃ) is His designator, and its repetition with contemplation leads to inward-turned consciousness and removal of obstacles. The chapter then lists the obstacles (antarāyas) and their accompaniments, and prescribes various practices to overcome them, including cultivation of attitudes (maitrī, karuṇā, etc.), breath control, sensory activity, meditation on inner light, and others. Finally, it explains the stages of samāpatti (coalescence) leading to sabīja samādhi.

story.title: Īśvara and the Path to Samādhi
story.arc: Exposition of the means to attain samādhi through Īśvarapraṇidhāna and other practices, with detailed description of obstacles and their remedies.
story.timeline_note: Timeless; the teachings are presented as eternal truths.
story.comic_potential: Low; the chapter is philosophical and instructional.

teachings.teaching: Surrender to Īśvara (Īśvarapraṇidhāna) is a means to attain samādhi.
teachings.lens_note: In Shailendra Sharma's Kriya-Yoga commentary, Īśvarapraṇidhāna is the key to transcending the ego and aligning with the cosmic will, leading to effortless meditation.

teachings.teaching: Īśvara is a special Puruṣa untouched by afflictions, karma, and their residues.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that Īśvara is the pure consciousness free from all conditioning, serving as the ideal for the practitioner to emulate.

teachings.teaching: The praṇava (Oṃ) is the designator of Īśvara; its repetition with contemplation leads to inward-turned consciousness and removal of obstacles.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma teaches that Oṃ is the primordial vibration; its japa purifies the mind and connects the practitioner to the divine source.

teachings.teaching: The obstacles to samādhi include disease, dullness, doubt, heedlessness, laziness, intemperance, false vision, non-attainment of stages, and instability.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma identifies these as distractions that arise from impure habits; they must be recognized and overcome through disciplined practice.

teachings.teaching: Cultivating friendliness, compassion, sympathetic joy, and equanimity brings serenity to the mind.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma interprets these attitudes as essential for harmonizing relationships and reducing mental agitation, preparing the mind for deeper meditation.

teachings.teaching: Through exhalation and retention of breath, serenity can be attained.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma links this to prāṇāyāma techniques that regulate the life force, calming the mind and facilitating concentration.

teachings.teaching: The arising of higher sensory activity can bring stability to the mind.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma explains that subtle perceptions (like inner light) can be used as objects of meditation to steady the mind.

teachings.teaching: Meditation on the inner light that is sorrowless and luminous brings stability.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma identifies this light as the inner self (ātman), which is ever blissful; focusing on it dissolves sorrow.

teachings.teaching: The mind can be stabilized by taking as object one whose passion has faded (a vītarāga).
teachings.lens_note: Sharma suggests contemplating a realized being to inspire detachment and focus.

teachings.teaching: Knowledge arising in dream and sleep can be a support for meditation.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma interprets this as using lucid dreaming or deep sleep awareness as a stepping stone to samādhi.

teachings.teaching: Meditation as one desires (yathābhimatadhyāna) is also a valid practice.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes the importance of personal inclination in choosing a meditation object, as it fosters natural absorption.

teachings.teaching: Mastery extends from the smallest atom to the largest.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma explains that through samādhi, the practitioner gains control over all levels of existence, from the subtle to the gross.

teachings.teaching: When modifications are diminished, there is coalescence (samāpatti) of knower, instrument, and known.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma describes this as the state where the mind becomes transparent, reflecting the object without distortion, leading to direct knowledge.

teachings.teaching: Savitarkā samāpatti is intermixed with constructs of word, meaning, and knowledge.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma notes that this is a preliminary stage where the mind still operates with conceptualization.

teachings.teaching: Nirvitarkā samāpatti occurs when memory is purified and consciousness shines as the object alone.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma explains that this is a higher stage where the mind is free from verbalization and directly intuits the object.

teachings.teaching: Savicārā and nirvicārā samāpatti have subtle objects and are explained similarly.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma indicates that these stages involve subtle elements and are progressively refined.

teachings.teaching: The subtlety of objects ends at the undifferentiated (aliṅga).
teachings.lens_note: Sharma identifies aliṅga as the unmanifested prakṛti, the subtlest object of meditation.

teachings.teaching: These forms of samāpatti constitute sabīja samādhi (samādhi with seed).
teachings.lens_note: Sharma explains that sabīja samādhi still has an object; it is the foundation for nirbīja samādhi (seedless).
